text: The surgeon places trocars, including one for the camera in the umbilicus, and four additional trocars, allowing access to all quadrants of the colon for mobilization.
The surgeon stands on the right side of the patient and incises the gastrocolic omentum from the middle to the inferior pole of the spleen.
The surgeon incises the anterior layer of the mesocolon at the inferior border of the pancreas, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon continues the division of the splenic flexure, using laparoscopic instruments to separate the colon from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon incises the posterior layer of the mesocolon at the duodenal jugular flexure, continuing the dissection while keeping the left side intact and separating it from the serratus fascia.
The surgeon performs an en bloc dissection, removing a section of tissue as a single unit, following previous mobilization and incisions around the colon.
The surgeon continues the dissection around the splenic flexure, maintaining separation of the mesocolon layers.
The surgeon carefully separates the mesocolon from the genitous fascia, revealing the underlying kidney and visualizing the pancreas.
The surgeon, positioned on the left side of the patient, turns attention to the right side of the surgical field to perform the next steps of the procedure.
The surgeon incises the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the second part of the duodenum beneath it.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum of the hepatocolic ligament, carefully identifying the Gerota's fascia beneath.
The surgeon dissects the second and third parts of the duodenum, carefully separating these sections from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon mobilizes the iliac cecum by incising the posterior inferior border of the mesentery, creating a tunnel for retropaternal dissection. The right side of the mesocolon is dissected up to the duodenum and the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon places the ileum and colon in their normal anatomical positions and incises the anterior layer of the mesentery along the border of the supramesenteric vein.
The surgeon divides the ileocolic vessels, first severing the vein followed by the artery, using surgical instruments to separate these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon divides the middle colic vessels, separating them from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the main vessels at the root of the mesentery near the inferior border of the pancreas.
The surgeon has completed the dissection, freeing the pancreas at its inferior border, with surrounding tissues visibly separated.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic vessel and the ileocolic vessel, highlighting their positions within the surgical field.
The surgeon releases the lateral attachment of the ascending colon, detaching it from surrounding tissues.
The right side of the colon, including the transverse colon up to the splenic flexure, is fully mobilized, indicating the completion of this stage of the dissection.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon and divides the inferior mesenteric vessels, including the arteries, as part of the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the left side of the sigmoid colon from the posterior layer, carefully preserving the ureter and gonadal vessels by keeping them positioned below the dissection plane.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to clear the geratous fascia from the mesocolon layer, navigating through the surrounding areolar tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection laterally towards the left abdominal wall and cranially, reaching the previously dissected area, ensuring the colon is fully mobilized.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the left side of the colon, following the previous dissection and division of the inferior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon incises the lateral border of the peritoneum, continuing the dissection process on the left side of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon completes the division of the peritoneum following the dissection of the left side of the colon, ensuring the ureter and gonadal vessels remain undisturbed.
The surgeon continues the peritoneal incision lateral to the colon, extending it upwards to meet the previously dissected area.
The descending colon is visibly freed from surrounding tissues, indicating successful completion of the lateral dissection up to the left abdominal wall.
The rectosigmoid junction is visible in the surgical field, showing the anatomical transition between the rectum and sigmoid colon.
The surgeon applies cranial traction to the colon, pulling it upwards to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon stretches out and incises the lateral peritoneal flap, revealing the underlying structures adjacent to the colon.
The surgeon works medial to the right ureter, continuing the dissection in the area adjacent to the previously freed descending colon.
The surgeon continues dissection through the areolar tissues, carefully maneuvering to protect the nearby anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies a clear dissection plane, with intact nerves visibly displayed within the surgical field.
The surgeon maintains the integrity of the mesorectum covering fascia anteriorly, ensuring it remains intact without any bridging.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection using a liver retractor, manipulating the tissue to maintain exposure and access.
The liver tanny is clearly visible during the dissection process.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the rectum up to the levator ani, applying pressure on the perineum to facilitate the division of the rectum from the anal canal at the levator ani level.
The surgeon completes the procedure by removing the entire rectum, which is now fully excised from the surgical field.
The divided staple line is visible, resembling the shape of a nose.
The surgeon identifies and divides the posterior vaginal wall, completing the structural division and moving towards the sutured end.
The surgeon introduces a valve system through a cone, using a grasper to manipulate the device within the surgical field.

A plastic sheath is inserted through the cone, extending from the peritoneal cavity to the outside. The surgeon introduces the divided end of the bowel through the open plastic sheath and slowly extracts it through the posterior vaginal wall.
The surgeon successfully extracts the entire bowel specimen through the post vaginal wall without difficulty.
The surgeon displays the extracted specimen, which is visible after being removed through the post vaginal wall.
The surgical field shows the total rectum, sigmoid colon, and part of the ileum prepared for extraction.
The surgeon folds the distal ileum to create a pouch, using a measured tape to ensure the pouch is precisely fifteen centimeters in length.
The surgeon joins the two loops of bowel together using interrupted sutures, securing the connection with a few stitches.
The surgeon divides the apex of the looped ileum, separating it from the rest of the intestinal structure.
The surgeon introduces a surgical stapler into the operative field, preparing to secure tissue or create an anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the jaws of the surgical instrument to position them into each bowel segment, overcoming initial difficulty.
The surgeon uses multiple cartridges to lengthen the pouch created from the folded distal ileum, visible through repeated application of the stapler.
The surgeon uses a six-centimeter echelon stapler to extend the pouch size, demonstrating the ease of manipulation and adjustment of the pouch diameter.
The surgeon adjusts the bowel over the stapler, utilizing the stitches in between to aid in proper alignment.
The surgeon introduces the anvil into the surgical field, preparing it for use in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon inserts the anvil into the prepared pouch, positioning it for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a Prolene suture to perform a bursting maneuver, likely to reinforce or secure the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a bursting technique to tie around the anvil, securing it in place within the surgical field.
The surgeon holds the anvil shaft with a specialized instrument while introducing the circular stapler through the anus, stapling the anvil and bowel together to create a circular anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the bowels for any rotation, ensuring proper alignment and positioning within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon removes the staple and inflates the bowel to check the integrity of the circular anastomosis. The tension of the vessels is assessed, confirming good alignment and no rotation.
The surgeon inspects the pouch for any signs of leakage, carefully examining the integrity of the anastomosis.
The surgeon identifies a loop of small bowel proximal to the pouch and uses it to create a split ileostomy on the right side of the abdominal wall.
